title: Training Data Requirements
concept: Because of their weak inductive bias, Vision Transformers require massive datasets (millions of images) to achieve or exceed the performance of CNNs.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference in inductive bias between CNNs and ViTs?

options.text: CNNs assume local pixel relations and translation invariance, while ViTs do not.
ViTs assume images are written in 1D, while CNNs assume 3D.

exercises.explain: Correct! CNNs have strong spatial priors, whereas ViTs learn relations entirely from the data.

cards.title: Feeding the beast
cards.body: If you feed a standard CNN and a ViT the same medium-sized dataset like ImageNet-1k (1.2 million images), the CNN will often win. Why? Because the ViT is like a giant beast that needs an enormous amount of food (data) before it can run properly.

cards.title: Data Scaling
cards.body: Training data scaling refers to how a model's performance improves as the size of the dataset grows. ViTs scale exceptionally well, meaning they continue to improve with massive data (e.g. 14M to 300M images) long after CNNs have plateaued.

cards.title: A glider vs. a rocket
cards.body: A CNN is like a glider: it takes off easily with a small push (small dataset) but has a limit on how high it can go. A ViT is like a heavy space rocket: it needs tons of fuel (data) just to lift off the ground, but once it gets going, it can fly all the way to orbit.

cards.title: JFT-300M Pre-training
cards.body: In the original ViT paper, when the model was trained only on ImageNet-1k, it underperformed ResNet. But when pre-trained on Google's private JFT-300M dataset (300 million images) and then fine-tuned, it set new state-of-the-art records.

cards.title: Not just for tech giants
cards.body: You might think you can never use ViT because you don't own 300 million images. Luckily, you can download a **pre-trained ViT** (trained by Google or Meta) and fine-tune it on your small custom dataset with excellent results!

cards.title: Performance vs. Dataset Size
cards.body: How data size changes model dominance:

left.text:
CNNs win easily
ViT struggles to generalise
CNN bias acts as a safety net

right.text:
ViTs dominate
CNNs plateau in accuracy
ViT capacity is unlimited

exercises.prompt: What happens when you train a standard ViT from scratch on a small dataset (e.g. 5,000 images)?
exercises.explain: Without massive data or pre-training, a ViT struggles to learn geometric structures, leading to poor generalization on small datasets.

options.text: It will likely overfit or perform poorly compared to a CNN because it cannot learn spatial relations well
It will automatically train 10x faster than on large data
It will achieve 100% accuracy immediately due to self-attention
It will convert itself into a ResNet

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model pre-trained on a massive dataset can be fine-tuned on a much smaller dataset.
exercises.explain: True. Pre-training teaches the ViT visual representations, allowing it to adapt to small datasets via fine-tuning.
ViTs have higher capacity and continue to improve as they are fed more data.
exercises.sentence: While CNN accuracy plateaus as dataset sizes grow, ViT models continue to ___ and scale.

exercises.prompt: Which dataset size represents the ideal training scenario for a ViT from scratch?
exercises.explain: ViTs excel when pre-trained on massive datasets containing hundreds of millions of images.

exercises.prompt: Why do CNNs plateau in accuracy on massive datasets while ViTs keep improving?

options.text: Because the local receptive field constraints of CNNs limit their capacity to learn complex global relationships, whereas ViTs have virtually unlimited representational capacity.
Because CNNs are not compatible with modern GPUs.
Because ViTs automatically discard background pixels.
Because CNNs suffer from too many classification heads.

exercises.explain: Brilliant! CNN's hardcoded assumptions restrict how much they can learn. ViT's open, attention-based layout means it can keep absorbing new complex patterns as long as you feed it data. Next: computational tradeoffs!
